coredata.dc:title: Electrospun polyacrylonitrile nanofibers as graphene oxide quantum dot precursors with improved photoluminescent properties
coredata.dc:creator: Zaca-Moran, O.
Sánchez-Ramírez, J.F.
Herrera-Pérez, J.L.
Díaz-Reyes, J.
coredata.dc:description: Graphene oxide quantum dots were synthesized from carbonization, ultrasonic peeling, and oxidation of electrospun polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers. By energy scattering X-ray spectroscopy, a strong increase/decrease in the carbon/oxygen concentration was found during the stabilization stage and during carbonization, which resulted in the formation of carbon-enrich nanofibers. FTIR spectroscopy and Raman scattering results of electrospun PAN nanofibers demonstrated changes in structural and optical properties during the carbonization process. By X-ray diffraction was found that after the carbonization and oxidation processes of the PAN nanofibers, they showed a hexagonal graphene structure with two main crystallographic directions (002) and (101). The formation of graphene oxide quantum dots resulting from the ultrasonic exfoliation process was demonstrated by TEM with nanocrystal sizes of 10.98 nm, which were obtained after the ultrasonic exfoliation of carbonized/graphitized PAN nanofibers in highly oxidizing solvents. The absorbance in the UV spectral region showed a band at approximately 265 nm, which was assigned to the π→π* excitation of the π bonds of aromatic CC, and another band at 325 nm that was assigned to the n→π* transition of CO bonds, showing the presence of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs). By Raman scattering is found that the synthesized GOQDs have a density of 22.6 ppm of crystalline defects, which indicates a higher order in their crystal lattices. The fluorescence and photoluminescence of the graphene oxide quantum dots showed radiative bands associated with possible crystalline defects in the graphene oxide quantum dot network, the radiative emissions of which correspond to the blue spectral region. The preparation protocol is simple and the yield of graphene oxide quantum dots is high.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrospun nanofibers
Graphene oxide
Quantum dots
Spectroscopic techniques

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PAN nanofibers and graphene oxide quantum dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Unidad Profesional Interdisciplinaria en Ingeniería y Tecnologías Avanzadas del Instituto Politécnico Nacional

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Consejo Nacional de Ciencia y Tecnología

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors wish to thank the Nanotechnology Postgraduate Program in Unidad Profesional Interdisciplinaria en Ingeniería y Tecnologías Avanzadas del Instituto Politécnico Nacional as well as CONACyT's programs that strengthen the scientific and human endeavour of one of the authors (O. Zaca-Morán: CONACYT/México grants No. 42228- F).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the electrospinning equipment used to synthesise the PAN nanofibers is presented.

It illustrates the schematic diagram of the GOQDs preparation using the ultrasonic exfoliation process of the carbonized PAN nanofibers [32]. Additionally, Colloidal dispersion photographic image of GOQDs is shown.

SEM micrographs obtained of PAN electrospun nanofibers: a) without heat treatment and b) carbonized at 1500 °C. In addition, in inset of Fig. 3b is shown a typical size distribution histogram for obtaining the mean diameter of the carbonized PAN nanofibers.
a) It shows the FTIR spectra of the nanofibers obtained from those stabilized and carbonized and b) It presents the X-ray diffraction pattern of nanofibers carbonized at 1500 °C.
It illustrates the Raman spectra of the nanofibers: a) stabilized at 270 °C and b) carbonized at 1500 °C. As well as the deconvolutions of the D and G bands in both Raman spectra.

a) The XPS spectra of the carbonized PAN nanofibers are presented. b)-c) They illustrate the XPS spectra deconvolutions to find the binding energies of the sp2 and sp3 hybridizations, as well as the CC and CO and COOH bonds.
The absorbance FTIR and Raman spectra of the QDs obtained from the carbonized and exfoliated PAN nanofibers are shown.
TEM and HRTEM micrographs of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) are shown.
It shows a) fluorescence and b) photoluminescence spectra of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs).

It presents the temperatures at that the PAN nanofibers were carbonized. In addition, the mean diameters of the carbonized nanofibers are shown and the molar fractions of the main elements are presented.

head.ce:title: Electrospun polyacrylonitrile nanofibers as graphene oxide quantum dot precursors with improved photoluminescent properties

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centro de Investigación en Biotecnología Aplicada del Instituto Politécnico Nacional
sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Km 1.5 de la Carretera Estatal Santa Inés Tecuexcomac-Tepetitla

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centro de Investigacion en Biotecnologia Aplicada del Instituto Politecnico Nacional. Ex-Hacienda de San Juan Molino. Km 1.5 de la Carretera Estatal Santa Ines Tecuexcomac-Tepetitla. , Tepetitla, Tlaxcala, 90700, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene oxide quantum dots were synthesized from carbonization, ultrasonic peeling, and oxidation of electrospun polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers. By energy scattering X-ray spectroscopy, a strong increase/decrease in the carbon/oxygen concentration was found during the stabilization stage and during carbonization, which resulted in the formation of carbon-enrich nanofibers. FTIR spectroscopy and Raman scattering results of electrospun PAN nanofibers demonstrated changes in structural and optical properties during the carbonization process. By X-ray diffraction was found that after the carbonization and oxidation processes of the PAN nanofibers, they showed a hexagonal graphene structure with two main crystallographic directions (002) and (101). The formation of graphene oxide quantum dots resulting from the ultrasonic exfoliation process was demonstrated by TEM with nanocrystal sizes of 10.98 nm, which were obtained after the ultrasonic exfoliation of carbonized/graphitized PAN nanofibers in highly oxidizing solvents. The absorbance in the UV spectral region showed a band at approximately 265 nm, which was assigned to the π→π* excitation of the π bonds of aromatic CC, and another band at 325 nm that was assigned to the n→π* transition of CO bonds, showing the presence of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs). By Raman scattering is found that the synthesized GOQDs have a density of 22.6 ppm of crystalline defects, which indicates a higher order in their crystal lattices. The fluorescence and photoluminescence of the graphene oxide quantum dots showed radiative bands associated with possible crystalline defects in the graphene oxide quantum dot network, the radiative emissions of which correspond to the blue spectral region. The preparation protocol is simple and the yield of graphene oxide quantum dots is high.

ce:section.ce:para: The idea of producing a new carbon allotrope has encouraged the research of new materials beyond fullerenes, nanotubes, nanodiamonds, graphene and quantum dots (QDs) with surprising results that provide the opportunity to obtain high-performance nanostructures, which has been the core of many studies of interest in carbon-based nanomaterials. The statistical bases show that this study type in recent years had exponential growth related to the synthesis and characterization, in particular, of carbon-based QDs (quantum graphene dots, GQDs, and graphene oxide quantum dots, GOQDs) due to their unique size (with lateral dimensions of <10 nm) [1,2], low toxicity [3–5] and good biocompatibility with relatively easy surface modification; additionally to the cheap carbon precursor, ease of synthesis and large-scale production of GOQDs. QDs of materials semiconductor are nanocrystals that exhibit quantum phenomena that can modify their optical properties [6]; however, the disadvantage is that they are generally synthesized with precursors such as selenium or cadmium, which are highly toxic and restrict their application in biology or medicine. On the other hand, the advantages obtained from the GQDs and GOQDs make them ideal for applications in biomedicine [7–9]. Since the discovery of graphene-based QDs, numerous research groups have studied and manufactured different methods with the aim of achieving the best preparation routes and a better understanding of their chemical (high stability photochemistry) and physical (optical and electronic) properties, which have wide applications in the development of new systems such as biological markers. These have emerged as superior and universal fluorophores in their own right due to their excellent photostability, easy functionality, small size, low toxicity, low molecular weight, favourable biocompatibility, chemical stability, and inert properties; they can also be adjustable, making them suitable for bioimaging and bioanalysis applications [10–14]. Until now, the production of graphene-based QDs has been explored from two different approaches: “top-down” and “bottom-up”. However, “bottom-up” approaches have serious drawbacks, such as special equipment and difficulty in controlling the GQD size. “Top-down” approach consists mainly of carbonization of organic precursors through thermal methods, which allows more precise control over the morphology and size distribution of the final products [15,16]. Nowadays, into this approach a large number of methods have been developed to produce graphene based GQDs or GOQDs on a large scale. However, the methodology that produces the best quality of GQDs is the mechanical exfoliation. In 2010, the possibility of controlling the number of graphite layers was first published, reaching several layers of graphene and suggesting the possibility of reaching one layer [17]. This methodology consists of manually exfoliation materials layers of graphite and was used to study the properties for the first time measure the topography of graphene [18]. Extrusion methods are another option to obtain graphene microfibers with a diameter greater than 10 μm [19], where one of the carbon precursors of the most used materials for more than thirty years has been polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and that is used to obtain GOQDs by ultrasonic exfoliation. In this context, carbon fibers with greater morphological control have recently been obtained through the electrospinning technique for different technological purposes. In the electrochemical field, Yang, et al. [20] investigated about a biosensor based on combined electrospun fibers graphene oxide (GO) QDs/ZnO for detection of intracellular H2O2 in cancer and normal cell. Recently, Tan et al. [21] published the production of quantum dots from electrospun polycaprolactone/CdSe fibers as a highly sensitive optical catheter for H2O2. The electrospun nanofibers have many qualities, such as high surface/volume ratio and 3D porous structures that make them ideal for exfoliation. For this reason, we have carried out for the first time a simple, fast and low-cost method, since it does not require expensive equipment and chemical reagents to obtain the GOQDs based on the carbonization/graphitization of nanofibers from a PAN solution; for which the following steps were required: electrospinning, stabilization, carbonization/graphitization as a precursor of fluorescent GOQDs, and ultrasonic exfoliation with highly oxidizing agents. In order to obtain graphene by the Hummer's method is used graphite oxide that is commercially acquired in most cases, which is treated with a solution of concentrated sulphuric acid (H2SO4), sodium nitrate (NaNO3) and potassium permanganate (KMnO4). In this work, the graphite oxide is obtained from the carbonization process of the PAN electrospun nanofibers, so the method becomes innovative and simple, in which the GOQDs size is controlled by controlling the morphology and diameter of PAN electrospun nanofibers, which contribute to the formation of more homogeneous nanoparticles during exfoliation, reducing the density of crystalline defects, which is measured through the ratio of carbon and oxygen (C/O). Using the Hummer's method, a ratio of 2.1–2.6 is obtained, for the present synthesis method this C/O ratio decreases to 1.6, which is manifested in the decrease in the concentration of crystal defects calculated by Raman spectroscopy for GOQDs [30]. The finding in this work promises many applications in these nanomaterials, where the combination of easy processing, sustainability and cost-effectiveness is required [22–25].

In this work, GOQDs were prepared through the ultrasonic exfoliation technique of carbonized/graphitized PAN nanofibers in oxidizing solvents. The electrospinning technique is an alternative for the preparation of nanofibers on the nanometric scale; its easy operation makes it ideal for reproducibly obtaining polymeric polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers that after being carbonized/graphitized and oxidized can be used as precursors of GOQDs with photoluminescent properties. To our knowledge, there is no report on the preparation of GOQDs using carbonized/graphitized electrospun nanofibers, so in this paper, the results of the preparation of GOQDs from the stabilization, carbonization/graphitization, oxidation and ultrasonic peeling of electrospun polyacrylonitrile nanofibers (PAN) are reported. The structural and optical properties of the stabilized, carbonized/graphitized and oxidized PAN nanofibers were characterized using SEM-EDS, FTIR, Raman scattering, as well as XRD and XPS. The preparation and characterization of the properties of the GOQDs by the ultrasonic exfoliation in oxidizing solvents of the carbon/graphite nanofibers were carried out using the UV–Visible, Raman, Fluorescence and Photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy techniques, and TEM and HRTEM microscopy.

All chemical materials were analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. Polyacrylonitrile (PAN) (C3H3N)n, with a purity of >99.96% and molecular weight of 150,000 g/mol), sulphuric acid (H2SO4, with a purity of >99.98%), nitric acid (HNO3, with purity >99.98%), were obtained from the Sigma-Aldrich company. N–N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, HCON(CH3)2, with a purity >99.8%), 2-Propanol (C3H8O with a purity >99.5%) were obtained from the company Mallinckradt Chemical AR. Deionized water (>18 MΩ cm−1) was used at all times to wash the laboratory equipment used in the preparation of PAN nanofibers and graphene oxide quantum dots.

The diagram of the electrospinning system that was used for the preparation of the PAN nanofibers is schematically presented in Fig. 1 . The equipment consists of an infusion pump (kd Scientific, KDS 100) used to control the feeding speed, which is connected to a stainless steel needle with an internal diameter of 0.51 mm through which the precursor PAN solution is expelled. A high-voltage source (EQ Matsusada Precision series) with two terminals connected to the terminal section of the stainless steel needle and the other connected to a metal collecting plate (aluminium foil 5.0 cm in diameter) where the nanofibers are deposited, these terminals are separated by a distance of 15 cm. When the electrical potential between the two electrodes is sufficient and exceeds the surface tension of the polymer solution, it emerges in the form of nanofibers towards the interconnected fiber network collector. Glass vial bottles with screw caps and BRANSON brand sonication equipment were used in the ultrasonic exfoliation process of carbonized/graphitized nanofibers for the preparation of graphene quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PAN nanofibers and graphene oxide quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: A polymeric solution was first prepared by dissolving PAN in 10 wt % DMF and subjected with rigorous stirring at room temperature for 72 h. Then, 2 ml of PAN solution was loaded into a 3 ml sensimedical syringe attached to a stainless steel needle and mounted to the infusion pump. The needle was then connected to the high voltage source operated in the positive DC mode with a voltage of 15 kV. The nanofibers were electrospun at a rate of 0.5 ml/h at room temperature for 15 min. The PAN nanofibers were deposited on a metal collecting plate. The obtained PAN nanofibers were then subjected to different heat treatments in a muffle furnace (SAFTherm brand, STM-3-18). Their stabilization was carried out at a temperature of 270 °C with a heating ramp of 5 °C/min for 30 min in a laboratory atmosphere and their carbonization/graphitization was carried out at 1500 °C with a heating ramp of 5 °C/min for 300 min in a nitrogen atmosphere [25–31]. 5 g of the thus obtained nanofibers were immersed in 120 ml of H2SO4 and 40 ml of HNO3 for 24 h at 100 °C for oxidation. In order to obtain the graphene oxide quantum dots, the nanofibers in H2SO4 and HNO3 were subjected to ultrasonic peeling at a frequency of 40 kHz for 2 h, and then the remaining solution was stirred for 24 h at 100 °C [32]. After being diluted with deionized water, the solution was adjusted to a neutral pH with NaOH and then the final product was obtained using an ultrafiltration equipment (MWCO 3000 and 10,000 Da) to remove the unwanted material, thus obtaining the GOQDs [26–31] that were immediately dispersed in 2-propanol, forming a GOQDs colloidal solution. The process of exfoliation and colloidal solution of the obtained graphene oxide quantum dots is shown in Fig. 2 .

The diameter and structural morphology of the nanofibers were characterized using a TESCAN VEGA TS 5136SB scanning electron microscope, with a resolution of 3.5 nm in high vacuum and a secondary electron detector (SE). Chemical stoichiometry was estimated by X-Ray Dispersive Energy Spectroscopy (EDS), which was performed with a JEOL JSM-IT300 electron microscope. The functional groups of the samples were identified by infrared spectroscopy through a Bruker Vertex 70 brand spectrometer (Bruker Optics Corporation, Billerica, USA) with Fourier transform, in ATR mode. Measurements were made in the mid infrared spectral region (400–4000 cm−1), with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and an integration time of 60 s (1 s/scan). Data acquisition and processing were performed using OPUS software, version 6.0 (Bruker Optics, USA). Raman scattering measurements were performed on a Thermo Scientific brand DXR Smart Raman spectrometer, with an operating range of 50–3350 cm−1 and a 780 nm laser excitation source with 12 mW output power and a CCD detector thermoelectrically cooled. X-ray diffraction was performed using a Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer with a CuKα radiation source (1.54 Å). Chemical analysis was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK) under ultra-high vacuum conditions (1 × 10−9 Torr pressure). The XPS system with a monochromatic AlKα1 X-ray source (hν = 1486.6 eV) is used to generate excitations of the samples. The binding energy of all elements was calibrated using the C–C bond at the peak of C 1s (284.8 eV). Extensive scans of the nanofibers with an energy step of 72 eV were taken in 5 scans. The GOQDs structural characterization was made by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) in a Tecnai G2 T20 TEM, operating at 300 kV. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) studies were carried out in a JEOL JEM200 of 80–200 kV; the obtained images are recorded with a CCD camera in real time. The Gatan Digital Micrograph software was used for the analysis of HRTEM images. High-resolution electron microscope images were digitally processed by using filters in the Fourier space. Optical absorption spectra of GOQDs colloid dispersions were recorded using a UV–Vis–NIR scanning spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, Evolution 600 model). The fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with an Ocean Optics USB 4000 spectrophotometer coupled to an optical fiber, with a 365 nm LED excitation source, with a Toshiba TCD1304 CCD detector, with a sensitivity of 130 photons per count at 400 nm, with pixel size of 8 μm × 200 μm and a resolution of 16 bit with detection range of 200–1100 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) was measured using a He–Cd (Omnichrome–Series 56) laser emitting at 325 nm with an optical excitation power of ~15 mW at room temperature. The radiative emission was focused to the entrance slit of a HRD-100 Jobin-Yvon double monochromator with a resolution better than 0.05 nm, and detected with an Ag–Cs–O Hamamatsu photomultiplier with a spectral response in the range 350–1000 nm. The energy position of the radiative transitions and the linewidth at half-maximum (FWHM) of each peak have been determined by a quantitative fit to the experimental PL spectra using a sum of Gaussian line distributions, the dominant peaks were first fit and the additional peaks were added as were necessary.

Fig. 3 illustrates the SEM micrographs of the electrospun PAN nanofibers as-synthesized and those carbonized at 1500 °C. From the SEM micrograph illustrated in Fig. 3a, the presence of solid nanofibres are clearly observed with a well-defined shape, a smooth surface and without the presence of interconnections and defects called beads, ribbon-like and pores through their longitudinal directions. Additionally, as-synthetized polymeric nanofibers with diameters at nanometric scale are evident showing the homogeneity of the electrospun nanofiber diameters. Based on measurements taken from the SEM micrographs of the samples a typical size distribution histogram was obtained (see inset in Fig. 3b), and in it can be detected a Gaussian adjustment with a narrow distribution of diameters and a mean diameter of the nanofibers is obtained, 211.82 ± 2.7 nm. A comparable analysis was performed for the rest of the samples, finding a particle diameter of 432.54 ± 24.0 nm for the as-synthesized sample. After stabilization (270 °C) and carbonization (1500 °C) heat treatments, the nanofibers showed a decrease in mean diameters of 432.54 ± 24.0 nm to 211.82 ± 2.7 nm, respectively, as are shown in Table 1 . Additionally, the SEM microscopy illustrates in Fig. 3b shows the surface of the carbonized PAN nanofibers with some pores, which one could assume to contribute to faster fractionation of the nanofibers during the exfoliation process. These diameter variations are a consequence of the loss of mass associated with the volatile impurities desorption as methane, hydrogen, hydrogen cyanide, water, carbon dioxide, ammonia and other gases [33] produced during heat treatment. EDS analyses were performed to quantify the molar fractions of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen in the different nanofibers as a function of the treatment temperature. Table 1 illustrates the percentages of the corresponding elements. For stabilized nanofibers is observed that the loss of nitrogen occurs with a decrease in oxygen molar fraction. For higher treatment temperatures in a nitrogen atmosphere, loss of oxygen and a progressive increase in carbon is evident, resulting in the formation of carbon-rich nanofibers. This result is consistent with the decrease in nanofiber diameter with the increase in treatment temperature observed in SEM micrographs. Consequently, nanofiber chemical compositions are highly dependent on treatment temperatures, which are illustrated in Table 1 [22,34].

By infrared spectroscopy, the chemical structure of the PAN nanofibers treated at different temperatures was analysed and the results are presented in Fig. 4 a. Regarding the infrared spectrum of the PAN nanofibers stabilized at 270 °C (Fig. 4a), in which prominent structural changes are observed in the intensity of the peaks related to OH's in the region of 2800–3000 cm−1, as well as of the aliphatic groups CH in 2200–2300 cm−1 attributed to the nitrile groups CN. At 1700 cm−1 there are signals related to the CO and N–H bonds, also changes in the amide groups are observed at 1680 cm−1. As well as the appearance of a peak at 1590 cm−1 associated with the mixture of CN and CC. Where the most important one is the conversion of CN to CN that is the result of cyclization and crosslinking that is the preparation of the chemical structure for the next step, which is the carbonization of the PAN nanofibers. As has been reported in the literature [34] the appearance of CC groups is the result of dehydrogenation. In addition, tension and vibration bands of the aliphatic groups (CH2 and CH3) are observed in 1450–1460 cm−1 and 1200-1360 cm−1, respectively, as well as bands associated with carbonyl in the range 1000–1100 cm−1 [35–42]. Finally, in the FTIR spectrum of the nanofibers carbonized at 1500 °C, see Fig. 4a, the vibrational bands at 2243 cm−1, as well as those associated with the CN bonds disappear, indicating the formation of carbon-rich nanofibers [37–42]. Fig. 4b shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of PAN nanofibres carbonized at 1500 °C, showing a dominant diffraction peak at 2θ = 24.85° and a weaker one at 2θ = 42.4°, which correspond to the crystalline planes (002) and (101) of a hexagonal graphite lattice (JCPDS, Card No. 75–1621), As is observed in Fig. 4b, the XRD pattern only shows the presence of graphite oxide obtained from the carbonization of PAN nanofibers, which is the precursor of GOQDs that are obtained after the exfoliation process of these carbonized nanofibers. Using the Bragg equation,
λ = 2 d s i n θ
, the interplanar distance d = 3.58 Å was found for the carbonized PAN nanofibers, which corresponds to the graphite belonging to the hexagonal crystalline phase with spatial group P63/mmc [3,43–47]. By refinement of the X-ray diffraction data, the parameters of the unit cell of the crystalline phase are found, taking into account that the crystal structure is hexagonal, giving for a = 2.58 Å and c = 7.16 Å. These values are close to those reported for bulk graphite [48], but present a slight unit cell tension in the Z direction.

Fig. 5 illustrates the Raman spectra of stabilized (a) and carbonized (b) electrospun PAN nanofibers, which present dominant vibrational bands at 1352 and 1582 cm−1, called D and G. As well as two additional vibrational bands, D* associated with the lattice vibrations corresponding to sp2-sp3 bonds and D″ that is caused by the distribution of impurities in interstitial sites of the lattice, which decreases when crystallinity increases, as well as the decrease in oxygen [49,50]. This behaviour suggests that is possible to consider the position of these bands as a good parameter to estimate the degree of graphene formation. The D and G bands are associated with active defects from the graphene structure as has been reported [50,51]. Where the vibrational band G is associated with the stretching movements of the bond in the plane of the pairs of carbon atoms, as well as in-plane vibrations for sp2 hybridization, which is a doubly degenerated active phonemic mode (optical transverse (TO) and optical longitudinal (LO)) with E2g symmetry in the centre of the Brillouin zone. While the phononic band D is associated with the stretching movements of the carbon rings, the intensity of this band is directly associated with the amount of crystalline defects present in the nanofibers, so the defects density in the structure of the graphite nanofibers could be determined directly by measuring the intensity ratio of bands D and G. An increase in the ratio of ID/IG intensities means an increase in the density of structural defects. The band D has been related to the disorder at the edge of the nanostructure due to the presence of sp3 hybridizations, as well as band G is related to the vibrations of the planes of carbon atoms due to sp2 hybridizations, associated with vibrations of the phonetic lattice [18,52,53], see Fig. 5b. In recent works, Cancado et al. [54] and Bruna et al. [55] have related the ratio of ID/IG intensities with the density n
D of the active Raman defects through the following relation that for λ = 780 nm used in the experiments leads to:
(1)
n D
(
c
m
− 2
)
= 2.16 ×
10 11
I D
/
I
G

For the honeycomb lattice of a graphene layer, the number of carbon atoms is calculated is
n C
= 3.82 ×
10 15
c
m
− 2
[56]. The concentration of Raman-active defects in ppm (parts per million) can be determined by taking the ratio
n D
/
n C
. From the Raman spectra shown in Fig. 5, using equation (1), it is obtained that the defect density in the nanofibers stabilized at 270 °C leads to
n D
= 4.963 ×
10 11
c
m
− 2
and
n D
/
n C
= 1.30 ×
10
− 4
= 130
p p m
. Similarly, for the sample carbonized at 1500 °C the ID/IG ratio is lower and therefore the defect density is lower, 75.2 ppm.

High-resolution XPS surface analyses of PAN nanofibers carbonized at 1500 °C are shown in Fig. 6 a. The following chemical elements could be found carbon (C 1s, 284.7 eV) and oxygen (O 1s, 532.7 eV). By deconvolution of XPS spectra of the sample carbonized at 1500 °C, the chemical bonds of the sp2 CC/C–C (284.5 eV) hybridizations could be mainly corroborated and with greater intensity and with a lower intensity sp3 C–O/C–N (285.2 eV). As well as the CO (287.4 eV) bonds that can be seen in Fig. 6b–c, which corroborate the functional groups observed in the FTIR spectrum obtained of the 1500 °C carbonized nanofibers [26,52,57,58]. As is seen in inset in Fig. 6b, these chemical elements showed a variation in their concentration depending on the heat treatment of the PAN nanofibers. From the XPS spectra of the analysed samples, a strong decrease was observed in the molar fraction of nitrogen, as well as in oxygen in the carbonized nanofibers, which favour sp2 and sp3 hybridizations. Furthermore, a strong increase in carbon concentration is observed due to the heat treatment at 1500 °C, as shown in inset in Fig. 6b. These observations validate the results of the FTIR analyses of the formation of carbon nanofibers, as well as those obtained by EDS spectroscopy [23,30,59,60].

In Fig. 7 a illustrates the absorption spectrum of non-exfoliated carbonized nanofibers that presents a band at 205 nm, which is associated with the characteristic π-π* transitions of aromatic C–C bonds [61]. In addition, Fig, 7a shows the absorption spectrum of the remaining colloidal dispersion obtained of the nanofibers after exfoliation by ultrasound of the carbonized and oxidized in presence of H2SO4 and HNO3, which presents two characteristic absorption bands between 265 and 325 nm that are attributed to the transitions of the carbonyl nuclei associated with the n-π* bonds from the surface of characteristic functional groups to graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) [62]. These are also associated with the n-π* absorption transitions in the –CO bonds, respectively, which have strong absorption in the UV region. The direct band gap energy (Eg) was determined using the Tauc's model of direct transitions with the optical absorption coefficient (α) obtained from optical transmittance spectra
α =
[
l o g
(
100 /
T o
)
]
/
[
τ l o g e
]
, which allowed finding two absorption edges 3.61 and 3.91 eV [63,64], values located between those obtained by the Hummer's method 2.9 and 4.4 eV [65], indicating a conjugate restructuring of graphene. These bands are due to the restructuring of the carbon atoms lattice to a sp2 hybridization, associating them with an increase in the electron concentration [20,34,63]. During ultrasound exfoliation, GQDs oxidation is preserved by generating oxygen-containing functional groups, including carbonyl, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy groups, which are introduced at the edges and on the basal plane during the exfoliation due to the presence of H2SO4 and HNO3, as shown in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 7b). These functional groups correspond to the region of 2931-2870 cm−1, 1734 cm−1 as well as 1460-1450 cm−1, 1380-1350 cm−1 and 1270-1220 cm−1 [35]. The presence of these functional groups also agrees with the characterization by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 7c) where a great change is observed between the D and G bands and the ID/IG ratio, giving a ratio of 22.6 ppm, indicating a higher order in the GOQDs crystal lattice as is seen in the HRTEM micrograph (Fig. 8 ) [18,20,38,57,64]. TEM images show that GOQDs have an average diameter of 10.98 nm. The HRTEM micrograph (Fig. 8b) shows the reticular fringes with an interplanar space of 0.19 nm of the crystal structure that corresponds to graphene fringes [26,32].

Fluorescence and photoluminescence of the GOQDs, whose spectra are shown in Fig. 9 . The GQDs oxidation has been reported to not only break the bonds between carbon atoms, but also disrupt the lattice causing functional oxygen groups to be bound to some centres of a carbon atom in the graphene plane. Consequently, the symmetry of the graphene sp2 is interrupted by the carbon sp3 hybridization giving rise to the confinement in the graphene oxide quantum points. These sp2 and sp3 bonds are determined by the π states of the aromatic rings associated with the existence of a constant energy difference between the emission and corresponding excitation between the π and σ orbitals of a low-energy photon. Because the π electron from the intermediate energy level towards the lowest energy unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), so subsequent relaxation in the highest energy occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) σ results in the emission of a longer wavelength it cuts emitting the photon that is fluorescence [54,60]. Two excitation bands were observed in the spectra, one at 436 (2.83 eV) and the other at 491 (2.52 eV) nm (Fig. 9a), these emissions are also attributed to the recombination of an electron-hole pair confined in clusters, which are assumed to be the luminescence centres located within a microstructure showing strong photoluminescence emission [2,52,66]. Confined excitons have also been reported within these quantum dots, forming excitons that are bound to structural defects as seen in photoluminescence spectra, where two luminescent bands at 2.65 and 2.94 eV can be observed, see Fig. 9b. The latter one could be due to the emission of photoluminescence from the smallest particles considered as quantum dots, while the radiative band at 2.65 eV could be associated with the largest particles where the emission could be related to excitonic confinement at the edges of some microstructure. The fluorescence emission in the quantum dots was excited with an UV lamp observing the characteristic blue colour of the GOQDs that in this investigation were obtained from the carbonized/graphized PAN nanofibers as previously shown in Fig. 2 [66–72].

Graphene oxide quantum dots were successfully obtained from carbonization/graphitization of electrospun PAN nanofibers. The FTIR analyses showed structural changes during the heat treatment of the electrospun PAN nanofibers, mainly in the CN, CO and CO bonds. XRD showed an interplanar distance associated with the presence of the graphite phase in the nanofibers treated at 1500 °C. The results of UV–Vis spectroscopy of the graphene quantum dots showed bands at 265 and 235 nm associated with the π-π* and n-π* bonds belonging to the characteristic aromatic rings of GOQDs. The XPS analyses showed a strong decrease in oxygen causing possible fluorescence due to oxidation during the exfoliation process. This florescence was observed with a UV lamp in its characteristic blue colour. The TEM, HRTEM and XRD results demonstrated the 10.98 nm nanometric size and crystal quality of the GOQDS. Obtaining the graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) could be achieved in a practical and simple way with improved luminescence properties using the ultrasonic exfoliation of electrospun and carbonized/graphitized PAN nanofibers, opening a wide possibility of applications in different areas of science and technology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the Nanotechnology Postgraduate Program in Unidad Profesional Interdisciplinaria en Ingeniería y Tecnologías Avanzadas del Instituto Politécnico Nacional as well as CONACyT's programs that strengthen the scientific and human endeavour of one of the authors (O. Zaca-Morán: CONACYT/México grants No. 42228- F).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Eda, Y. Y. Lin, C. Mattevi, H. Yamaguchi, H. A. Chen, I. S. Chen, C. W. Chen, M. Chhowalla. Blue Photoluminescence from Chemically Derived Graphene Oxide. Adv. Mater. 22 (2010) 505-509.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semiconductor quantum dots for biosensing and in vivo imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xing, Z. Xia, J. Rao. Semiconductor quantum dots for biosensing and in vivo imaging. IEEE Trans Nanobioscience 8 (2009) 4-12.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioconjugated quantum dots for cancer research: present status, prospects and remaining issues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Biju, S. Mundayoor, R. V. Omkumar, A. Anas, M. Ishikawa. Bioconjugated quantum dots for cancer research: Present status, prospects and remaining issues. Biotechnol. Adv. 28 (2010) 199-213.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Puvvada, B. N. P. Kumar, S. Konar, H. Kalita, M. Mandal, A. Pathak. Synthesis of biocompatible multicolor luminescent carbon dots for bioimaging applications. Sci. Technol. Adv. Mater. 13 (2012) 045008.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Zhang, X. Zhao, Y. Ji, Z. Ouyang, X. Wen, J. Li, Z. Su, G. Wei. Electrospinning graphene quantum dots into a nanofibrous membrane for dual-purpose fluorescent and electrochemical biosensors. J. Mater. Chem. B 3 (2015) 2487-2496.
